content.assessment: Maintaining the current three-stage selection funnel (ages 10, 12, 14) preserves the psychological conditions associated with strong reference-group and self-concept effects. The robust big-fish-little-pond literature indicates that early and repeated selection concentrates high performers into selective streams while leaving non-selected students in comparatively lower-status environments, with implications for aspiration and academic self-concept that compound over three transition points rather than one. Monitoring without structural change does not alter the underlying selection mechanism, so any labeling or expectancy dynamics already embedded in track placement (contested in magnitude, potentially larger for low-SES students) would continue unaddressed. From a learning-mechanism standpoint, this proposal introduces no new cognitive-load, motivation, or self-regulation mechanisms — it simply continues status quo dynamics rather than testing an alternative.
opportunities: Systematic monitoring could eventually surface data distinguishing context-dependent from robust psychological effects, improving evidence quality for future reform
strengths: Avoids abrupt structural disruption that could itself be a source of transitional stress or cognitive-load spikes for students mid-track
Monitoring can generate data on self-concept and motivation trends that could inform future timing decisions
threats: Perceived policy avoidance could itself undermine student and family trust in the system's fairness, indirectly affecting motivation and engagement, though this pathway is speculative from this lens
weaknesses: Continued three-stage selection funnel preserves conditions linked to reference-group effects that lower self-concept among equally able students outside top tracks
No mechanism to mitigate any labeling or expectancy effects that may already be operating on stigmatized or low-SES students within the current sorting structure
